Title: Azalina: Law amended to curb transnational child sexual abuse
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Amendments to the Sexual Offences against Children Act are aimed at strengthening legal protection against child sexual exploitation, particularly offences committed across borders.
Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said said the move is in line with efforts to enhance protection for children against cross-border sexual crimes, while ensuring the Act remains relevant amid the evolving landscape of sexual offences.
She said the legislation was necessary to keep pace with the evolving nature of child sexual crimes, which have become increasingly sophisticated due to advances in digital technology, social media, messaging applications and online platforms.
She said although the Act had established a robust legal framework, experience from its implementation showed that provisions on extraterritorial jurisdiction needed to be strengthened to better address crimes committed beyond Malaysia's borders.
"The landscape of crime has changed significantly. Technological developments have created new avenues that are being exploited by perpetrators of sexual crimes against children," she said when tabling the amendments at the second reading.
Azalina cited findings from the Disrupting Harm in Malaysia report, which estimated that more than 100,000 Malaysian internet users aged between 12 and 17 had experienced online sexual exploitation or abuse within a year before the study was conducted.
She also highlighted Internet Watch Foundation data showing that 16,238 reports involving child sexual abuse material (CSAM) linked to Malaysian children were recorded in 2024, representing an increase of more than 225 per cent from the previous year.
In the first six months of 2025, another 12,656 reports were recorded, underscoring the growing threat posed by online child sexual exploitation.
Azalina said police had also reported that online child sexual offences had become increasingly complex, with offenders using encrypted platforms, false identities and operating across multiple jurisdictions, making investigations and evidence gathering more challenging.
Under the proposed amendments, the scope of Section 3 of the Act will be expanded to strengthen Malaysia's extraterritorial jurisdiction.
Currently, offences committed outside Malaysia can only be prosecuted under the Act if the offender is a Malaysian citizen.
The amendments will extend the law's application to permanent residents and individuals ordinarily resident in Malaysia who commit such offences abroad.
Legal protection will also cover child victims who are Malaysian citizens, permanent residents or ordinarily resident in Malaysia.
Azalina said the expanded jurisdiction would ensure more comprehensive legal protection in line with the increasingly transnational nature of child sexual offences.
She said the amendments also send a clear message that Malaysia would not compromise on any form of sexual exploitation involving children.
"If you touch our children, you go.
"There will be no tolerance and no compromise for those who destroy the future of our children," she said.
She added that the government would continue strengthening international cooperation and domestic coordination through a whole-of-government approach involving enforcement agencies, prosecutors, child protection authorities, cybersecurity agencies and communications regulators to ensure the law is effectively enforced.
A total of 25 MPs are debating the bill in the Dewan Rakyat today.
The bill was
tabled
for first reading on June 23.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran, Oman hold first Hormuz talks since US war deal
Article:
TEHERAN: Iran said Monday it had held its first meeting with Oman on managing the Strait of Hormuz since it signed a preliminary deal to end the Middle East war with the United States.
The announcement followed the US saying it had agreed with Iran to halt attacks after an exchange of strikes strained their deal, with the two sides planning to renew talks to end the conflict.
A series of exchanges of fire has underscored the fragility of the Pakistan-brokered agreement to stop the war, which sowed havoc across the Middle East and snarled the flow of oil and gas shipments through the vital Hormuz strait.
Iran and Oman border the strait, through which a fifth of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas passed prior to the conflict. Teheran blockaded Hormuz to gain leverage over its foes, and it remains a key sticking point in the negotiations with the US.
"During a trip to Muscat, the first meeting of the Joint Hormuz Committee was held," said Iranian Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi on X. "While reviewing the current issues related to the strait, we exchanged views on the future management."
The announcement came hours after a US official told AFP that technical talks with Iran would continue.
"Both sides will stand down for now and vessels can move freely" in and around the strait, the official said in an email.
Iran did not immediately comment on the US statement, and the US official did not confirm a US media report that talks would resume Tuesday in Qatar.
Iran had on Sunday warned that any attempt by ships to bypass its preferred route through Hormuz would "increase tensions" in the Middle East.
The strait comprises Omani and Iranian territorial waters, but under customary international law the two cannot generally block passage or charge tolls.
Teheran's continued exercise of control over the strait has sparked repeated flare-ups, the latest of which came early Sunday, when US Central Command said it had attacked 10 Iranian military targets over "continued Iranian aggression against commercial shipping."
Iran said it retaliated with strikes against US bases in Kuwait and Bahrain. Both Kuwait and Bahrain denounced the Iranian attacks.
- 'HEGEMONIC DREAMS' -
Iran insists ships transiting the strait pass through a corridor near its own shores, though this week dozens of vessels have travelled along the opposite side of the waterway, hugging the Omani coast.
"Any attempt to adopt new or separate arrangements compared to what is underway by the Islamic Republic of Iran, will only lead to more complicated situations and delays in the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, and will increase the tensions," Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said.
The published text of the memorandum of understanding says Iran will define the future administration of the strait in dialogue with Oman and the other Gulf States, but "in line" with international law.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards said they were taking measures to control traffic in the strait and that vessels violating those measures would be dealt with more firmly than before.
Mohammad Mokhber, adviser to Iran's supreme leader, wrote on X that as long as Iran managed the strait, Washington's "hegemonic dreams in the region will not be realised."
Experts said there would likely be more Hormuz incidents.
For Iran, "a drawn-out negotiation accompanied by controlled pressure in the strait can work to its advantage", said HA Hellyer of the Royal United Services Institute, a London think tank.
While the exchanges have largely been without reported casualties, Qatar's interior ministry said one of its citizens was killed aboard a boat by shrapnel from "military operations in the area."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor Election: BN unfazed by party-hopping allegations in Endau
Article:
MERSING: Allegations of party hopping played up by opponents have not affected grassroots support for Barisan Nasional (BN) candidate for the Endau seat, Alwiyah Talib.
Johor BN chairman Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi said this was based on the positive reception from the grassroots towards Alwiyah after her return to BN under the "Rumah Bangsa Johor" initiative.
He said Alwiyah had previously demonstrated her commitment as a leader who consistently worked for the people, even when she was in the opposition.
"We are grateful for the Rumah Bangsa Johor concept initiated by the president (Umno president Datuk Seri Ahmad Zahid Hamidi), and at the grassroots level, they have accepted her back into BN.
"We all know she is a good leader.
"Even when she was in the opposition before, she consistently carried out her responsibilities for the people," he said after the Onn X Onn programme here today.
Meanwhile, Onn Hafiz assured full support for Alwiyah to ensure BN retains the Endau state seat in the Johor election.
He said the party machinery, including Umno, MCA, MIC as well as the Youth, Wanita and Puteri wings, would be mobilised to reach out to voters.
"I give my assurance to Wanita, Youth, Puteri, MCA, MIC and Umno that I will give full support to her.
"We still have two weeks. If everyone here goes out to vote and helps with the campaign, we can achieve victory," he said.
He said BN's victory in Endau also depends on support from all communities, as the strength of Bangsa Johor lies in its multiracial harmony.
"In Endau, there are more than 1,000 Orang Asli, around 4,000 Chinese voters, about 21,000 Malays and around 200 Indians.
"We must move together because our strength lies in unity," he said.
In the 2022 Johor election, Alwiyah, then representing Perikatan Nasional (PN), defended the Endau seat with a 3,041-vote majority in a five-cornered contest.
The Endau seat, with 28,767 registered voters, will see a four-cornered fight involving Alwiyah, Saiful Nizam Samat of Pakatan Harapan, PN's Hasnul Hakimi Hussien and Jati Awang of Parti Orang Asli Malaysia.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor polls: PH candidate claims campaign sabotage in Tanjung Surat
Article:
KOTA TINGGI: Pakatan Harapan (PH) Tanjung Surat candidate Faizul Abdul Ghani has lodged a police report and submitted a complaint to the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) over an alleged fake Facebook account and claims of campaign interference.
He said a Facebook page using the name "Harapan Pengerang" is not an official account linked to him, his party machinery or PH at the constituency level.
He said the account had allegedly published content that created confusion among voters, including posts relating to an incident involving the
burning of a PH flag
in the constituency.
"We have lodged police and MCMC reports, as well as complaints to the Election Commission, as it risked misleading the public and damaging the integrity of the campaign," he said in a statement today.
He also alleged that his campaign materials, including billboards, had been obstructed by the placement of party flags in front of them in several locations.
Faizul called for election campaigning to focus on policies, track records and development proposals instead of attempts to create confusion or disrupt campaign materials.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor polls: Young voters seek fresh ideas, others value experience
Article:
JOHOR BARU: As Johor heads to the polls on July 11, will first-time voters back fresh political faces or place their trust in experienced incumbents?
For one household in Nusa Idaman, the answer depends on who you ask.
Despite living under the same roof, three members of the family — representing two generations of voters — hold contrasting views on the qualities that matter most when choosing the state's next elected representatives.
Tourism industry player Sheikh Ahmad Fauzi Sheikh Abu Bakar, 54, believes the time has come for younger leaders to step forward.
"As a father, I feel younger candidates should be given the opportunity to prove themselves.
"In my line of work, I've met many young people, some as young as 19, doing extraordinary things. They are the voice of the future.
"Malaysia has changed significantly since the Covid-19 pandemic. Young people may not always be outspoken, but they are capable of delivering results.
"They may communicate differently, but what matters is getting the job done," he said.
His wife, Khalilah Abdul Rasip, 56, who is a lawyer, takes a more cautious approach, saying experience remains an important consideration.
"I was among those who believed it was time for a change in government in 2018. Looking back, I've come to appreciate the value of stability.
"I'd rather go with the devil I know and hope for the best," she said.
Their only child, Sheikh Umar Mukhtar, 22, who will be casting his ballot for the first time, said age alone would not determine his choice.
"I prefer younger politicians, whether they are incumbents or first-time candidates.
"I think the current Johor government has performed well, but every organisation needs fresh ideas.
"New faces prevent complacency and encourage better performance. It's like playing an online game, where tougher challenges help you improve," he said.
Although they live under the same roof, their differing political views are reflected in where they will cast their ballots.
The family will vote in three different state constituencies.
Khalilah will vote in Kampung Pasir, Fauzi in Tenggaroh and Umar will cast his first ballot in Perling.
While younger voters account for a smaller share of Johor's electorate than older age groups, they remain an influential demographic that BN, PH, Perikatan Nasional (PN), Muda and Bersama have actively courted through social media campaigns, youth-centred policies and younger candidates.
According to the Election Commission (EC), Johor has 2.73 million registered voters, with those aged between 21 and 29 making up almost 20 per cent of the electorate.
Across the state, political parties have adopted different strategies, retaining experienced assemblymen in some constituencies while fielding professionals, community leaders and first-time candidates in others.
Political pundits said first-time voters should not be viewed as a homogeneous bloc, noting that their decisions are likely to be shaped more by issues affecting their daily lives than by traditional party loyalties.
Employment opportunities, affordable housing, the rising cost of living, public transport and digital connectivity are expected to rank among the key issues influencing younger voters.
At the same time, incumbency continues to carry weight, particularly in constituencies where elected representatives have established strong grassroots networks and a proven track record of constituency service.
Pundits said the outcome of the July 11 polls would indicate whether younger voters favour political renewal or continue to place greater value on experience, continuity and local performance.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Court throws out RM9.13mil suit over historic Terengganu waqf land
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A 16-year fight to reclaim the historic Kampung Ladang Tok Pelam land in Terengganu ended when the High Court threw out a suit by a descendants' group seeking the return of the property or RM9.13 million in compensation.
Judge Yusrin Faidz Yusoff, in dismissing Pertubuhan Dansanak Haji Awang Bin Chik's originating summons held that the society had no legal standing to sue the state government and the Terengganu Islamic Religious and Malay Customs Council (Maidam).
The court also ruled that the plaintiff had filed the wrong type of action, brought its claim too late, and raised issues that fell within the exclusive jurisdiction of the Syariah Court.
The plaintiff had claimed the land was wakaf zuriat (family waqf) belonging to the descendants of Tok Pelam, one of Terengganu's most revered Islamic scholars, and sought either the return of the land or RM9,133,430.27 paid by the state to the Maidam after the land was compulsorily acquired in 2010.
The acquisition paved the way for the redevelopment of Kampung Ladang Tok Pelam, whose demolition in 2011 sparked public controversy over the loss of one of Terengganu's oldest Malay settlements.
The judge said the plaintiff, registered only in December 2024, had no proprietary interest in the land and therefore lacked locus standi to bring the action.
"The society was not the registered proprietor, nor was it the beneficiary of any trust over the land.
"The mere fact that some of its members may claim to be descendants of Tok Pelam does not vest any property right in the society itself," he said in his 29-page grounds of judgment dated yesterday (June 28).
The judge also found that even if the action had been brought by the alleged descendants personally, none had obtained letters of administration or probate to represent the estates of the original landowners.
He further ruled that the claim, filed in September last year, came well after the 12-year limitation period had expired, noting that the acquisition was completed in 2010 while the plaintiffs claimed they were dispossessed in 2011.
"The action filed in 2025 is therefore 14 years too late," he said.
Yusrin also held that the suit was procedurally flawed as it was commenced by way of an originating summons, even though it involved complex disputes over land acquisition, constitutional rights and waqf law.
The judge said the civil court lacked jurisdiction to determine whether the land was wakaf zuriat or whether Maidam was its lawful trustee, saying those were matters reserved for the Syariah Court.
On the merits, the court found that the compulsory acquisition was lawfully carried out under the Land Acquisition Act.
The court held that Maidam, as the statutory sole trustee of waqf property in Terengganu, was entitled to receive the RM9.13 million compensation.
The court accepted Maidam's evidence that the compensation was used to acquire nine replacement waqf properties under the Islamic concept of istibdal for the benefit of Tok Pelam's legitimate descendants.
The judge also held that while the Syariah High Court had identified 855 descendants entitled to benefit from the family waqf, the plaintiff's members were not among those recognised.
"If the plaintiff's members believe they are legitimate descendants, their remedy lies in the Syariah Court, not in the civil court."
The High Court dismissed the suit and ordered the plaintiff to pay RM10,000 in costs to each defendant.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump's Iran deal falls short for some voters, could cost Republicans the midterm
Article:
PRESIDENT Donald Trump's interim agreement to end the war with Iran has dragged down his approval rating and garnered criticism across the political spectrum — even from his own supporters.
Recent interviews with 18 Americans who voted for Trump in 2024, a group that Reuters has interviewed monthly since he returned to office, show that most have doubts about the deal, which has reopened the Strait of Hormuz while temporarily lifting US oil sanctions on Iran and authorising a US$300 billion fund for its reconstruction.
"We need to truly weaken the Iranian regime instead of this, 'beat them up a little bit and then step back and let them rebuild'," said Terry Alberta, 65, a pilot in Michigan.
Overall, only a quarter of Americans believe the war with Iran was worth the costs, ‌and a majority worry that the truce with Teheran is unlikely to last, according to the latest Reuters/Ipsos poll.
Many of the Trump voters feared his unpopular concessions to Iran would make it harder for Republicans to retain control of Congress in November's midterm elections, although those most critical of the deal had already begun to lose faith in the president before the war.
Six in the group believed he still had plans to bring down the Iranian government.
The group largely supported the war during its early days, believing US strikes were necessary to deplete Iran's stockpile of long-range missiles and cripple its nuclear program.
[[nid:1475948]]
Nearly four months later, with Iran politically emboldened and many of its military capabilities still intact, 14 of the voters criticised some aspects of the memorandum of understanding announced on June 14.
Most were skeptical that Teheran could be trusted to honour any agreement and dismayed by the prospect of granting it billions of dollars to rebuild.
The US$300 billion fund will be a private investment vehicle rather than a government-funded plan, though exact details have not been released.
Juan Rivera, 26, said Trump "criticised his predecessors about negotiating with terrorists, and he's basically done the same exact thing."
Rivera still plans to support mostly Republican candidates in the midterms.
But he said that when he volunteered recently to canvass Latino voters in his community near San Diego, many fellow Trump supporters were so disappointed by the president's handling of the war, among other issues, that they felt unmotivated to back his party in November.
"A lot of people say: 'Why should I vote when the president's not doing what he promised?'" Rivera recalled.
Asked for comment, a White House spokesperson told Reuters that Trump's achievement "on the battlefield and at the negotiating table is nothing short of remarkable and will strengthen American security for many years."
Steve Egan, 65, a promotional product distributor in Tampa, soured on Trump in early 2025 after tariff-triggered price hikes hurt his business.
From the outset, Egan was skeptical of the president's rationale for the war and upset that it further jacked up the price of gas and other goods.
[[nid:1475947]]
"Right now it doesn't seem like it's been worth it to go through all that," he said, noting that the stated goal of regime change "didn't happen."
His opinion of the president is now so low, Egan said, that Trump's endorsement would be "the kiss of death" for him when deciding which candidates to vote for in the midterms.
Brandon Neumeister, 37, a Pennsylvania state corrections worker and former National Guardsman,  said the conflict seemed only to have benefited oil companies.
Even before the war, though, Neumeister said he was unlikely to vote in November because he was disgusted with politics.
Robert Billups, 35, of Washington state, was cautiously optimistic the peace deal would hold. But he believed the war had spawned more hostility toward the United States rather than making the country safer.
Vice President JD Vance, tasked with leading US negotiations with Iran, has fallen in his esteem, and Billups said he no longer feels preferential toward Republican candidates.
Come November, "whoever has a better strategy this time, I'm gonna vote for them regardless of their party," he said.
Though Trump has been adamant about wanting to end the war, six of his more loyal voters expressed hope that he still had secret plans to bring Iran to heel.
[[nid:1475946]]
Kate Mottl, 63, a secretary at a municipal office in the Chicago suburbs, said that "destroying" the regime in Teheran seemed like the only way to avert future conflict.
It would be "very disappointing" if Trump refrained from further military intervention, Mottl said, adding that she believed "there's a bigger plan here."
Rich Somora, 62, an engineer in North Carolina, agreed that Trump probably had more aggressive plans up his sleeve.
"I can't imagine that he would have gone through all this and not found out a way to get rid of those mullahs," he said.
According to diplomats and analysts, however, the war has only strengthened the grip of Iran's clerical rulers.
If they remain in power for another month, Somora said, he'll start to worry.
In Prescott, Arizona, 74-year-old retiree Joyce Kenney said she supported lifting sanctions and believed restoring Iran's ability to trade with other countries would ensure its leaders honoured the truce.
But the reconstruction fund was a bridge too far: "That's not our responsibility," she said.
* The writer is from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: State election unlikely to derail Johor home sales
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Johor's state election on July 11 is unlikely to have a lasting impact on the state's property market, with any temporary slowdown in home sales expected to be offset in the months that follow.
Juwai IQI co-founder and group chief executive officer Kashif Ansari said some buyers may delay purchases until after polling day, but the full-year market outlook remains intact based on historical trends and current transaction patterns.
"I advise consumers that Johor home sales might slow in the weeks around the July 11 vote.
"However, they will then bounce back, with the full year not affected at all," he said in a statement.
Ansari said the conclusion was based on transaction data, previous election cycles and buyer enquiries received by the group's sales teams.
He noted that during 2022, when Johor held a state election in March and Malaysia held a general election in November, residential transactions in the state still increased by more than 25 per cent.
Meanwhile, total transaction value rose by nearly 30 per cent, helped by the reopening of the Singapore border.
Johor also outperformed during the 2013 general election year, recording about 16 per cent growth in residential transactions despite an overall decline in housing transactions nationwide.
"For 2026, some buyers may choose to wait until the state vote is over, which could reduce transactions in the current quarter.
"But once the election is over, the buyers who held off will get back into the market and make up for lost time," he said.
Ansari said preliminary data from the National Property Information Centre (Napic) showed Johor recorded 8,730 residential transactions worth RM4.45 billion in the first quarter of 2026, a 9.5 per cent decline in transaction volume from a year earlier.
He attributed the softer performance partly to weaker foreign demand following the doubling of Johor's foreign-buyer stamp duty in January, particularly among buyers from Singapore and China.
However, he expects foreign demand to recover by the end of the year, arguing that Johor homes remain significantly more affordable than comparable properties across the Causeway.
According to IQI, the average home price in Johor stood at RM487,128 in the first quarter, compared with RM2.1 million for an average resale HDB flat in Singapore.
The temporary pullback in overseas buyers also shifted demand towards more affordable housing, with homes priced at RM500,000 and below accounting for about 63 per cent of residential transactions during the quarter.
Properties priced below RM300,000 made up almost 38 per cent of total transactions.
Terraced houses remained the most popular housing segment, with one-, two- and three-storey units collectively accounting for more than half of all residential transactions in Johor.
Over the medium to longer term, Ansari said structural growth drivers, rather than political developments, will determine the direction of Johor's property market.
He cited the upcoming opening of the Singapore Rapid Transit System (RTS) Link and the continued development of the Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone (JS-SEZ) as key catalysts expected to support economic growth, cross-border demand and housing affordability.
"If I go up to 30,000 feet and look down at Johor, I can see that it is still one of Malaysia's strongest residential markets on a fundamentals basis and has much growth ahead of it.
"That growth will be supported by cross-border demand, new infrastructure like the RTS link, and a substantial pipeline of new affordable and mid-priced home construction," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia Airlines raises ticket prices by 20 to 30pct amid soaring fuel costs
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia Aviation Group (MAG) is strengthening its fuel hedging strategy and expanding its fleet as part of efforts to mitigate rising operational costs driven by volatile global fuel prices.
President and group chief executive officer Captain Nasaruddin A. Bakar said fuel currently accounts for about 30 per cent of Malaysia Airlines' operating costs, but this had risen to around 50 per cent following geopolitical tensions since late February.
"First and foremost, fuel costs for Malaysia Airlines are about 30 per cent.
"However, after the conflict that took place around Feb 28, fuel prices increased to about 50 per cent," he said during a corporate visit by MAG to Media Prima Bhd here today.
He added that the airline has implemented fuel hedging, which helps cushion around 36 per cent of the impact from fuel price fluctuations.
"Fuel hedging helps us mitigate the impact of rising prices. We also align our ticket pricing with prevailing market rates together with other airlines in the region and globally," he said.
Nasaruddin said Malaysia Airlines has adjusted fares by about 20 to 30 per cent in line with industry trends.
"Fare adjustments are in line with other airlines, both regionally and globally. We have increased fares by around 20 to 30 per cent," he said.
On route performance, he said demand had remained strong, particularly on long-haul routes to Europe such as London, prompting the airline to increase flight frequency based on market demand.
"We saw strong demand, especially in Europe, particularly London. Based on demand at the time, we increased frequencies," he said.
He added that current load factors averaged around 84 per cent, with some sectors reaching up to 95 per cent.
Looking ahead, Nasaruddin said MAG is focusing on cost control and operational efficiency, including fleet modernisation.
"We are managing operating costs, particularly fuel efficiency. We have ordered 95 new aircraft, with about 27 already delivered," he said.
He said the new aircraft would improve fuel efficiency by 12 to 15 per cent, depending on the model, supporting the group's long-term cost optimisation strategy.
Led by Media Prima group managing director Datuk Rafiq Razali, the corporate visit aimed to strengthen collaboration between MAG and Media Prima as part of the airline's efforts to foster closer ties with its media partners.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trainocate marks 30 years with AI skills programme for underserved youth
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  Trainocate Malaysia marks its 30th anniversary by launching an initiative to equip underserved youths with practical artificial intelligence (AI) skills to improve their employability.
The IT and professional development training provider said the TrAInocate Career Jumpstart 30 programme trained 116 youths from welfare homes, community organisations and community colleges across three cohorts throughout June.
The programme concluded with a graduation ceremony attended by the Raja Muda of Selangor, Tengku Amir Shah Sultan Sharafuddin Idris Shah,
It was conducted in collaboration with the Selangor Youth Community (SAY) and supported by MyDigital Corporation.
The initiative sought to bridge the gap between digital literacy and workplace readiness by providing participants with practical, job-related AI skills.
Training covered four areas: the use of generative AI tools to produce resumes, cover letters and professional communications; research and analysis using platforms such as Google Gemini; AI-assisted visual design through Canva AI; and integrating multiple AI tools into workflow processes.
SAY chief executive officer Aiman Syafiq Nazri said the programme was intended to widen access to opportunities in the digital economy.
"At SAY, our mission is to ensure that every young Malaysian, regardless of background, has the opportunity to thrive in today's economy," he said.
"TrAInocate Career Jumpstart 30 goes beyond teaching technology. It equips young people with practical skills, confidence, and exposure to opportunities that can help them pursue careers in the digital and technology sectors."
Trainocate Malaysia managing director Ruby Kaur said the initiative was a fitting way to commemorate the company's three decades of operations.
"AI literacy is rapidly becoming an essential employability skill. By partnering with organisations such as MyDigital Corporation and SAY we hope to ensure that these newly skilled young people are connected to emerging opportunities within Malaysia's growing digital economy," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Two Malaysians identified as injured in Thailand roadside bomb blast
Article:
KOTA BARU: Two Malaysians have been identified as being injured after a roadside bomb exploded beneath a culvert along Highway 42 in Kampung Phrai Wan near Tak Bai sub-province today, in an update from earlier reports that mentioned only one victim.
They were identified as Abdullah Syarapi Abd Rahman, 45, and Muhammad Yusri Udin, 38.
Narathiwat provincial police commander Major General Prayong Kotsakha said Abdullah, who is from Kelantan, suffered shrapnel wounds to his forehead, a serious injury to his left leg and head injuries.
Yusri, from Sabah, sustained minor injuries.
He said the explosion occurred about 11.41am (Thai time) at Kampung Phrai Wan, located about 100m from the Sapom three-way junction on the Narathiwat-Tak Bai Highway.
He said the victims, who were travelling in a Malaysian-registered vehicle, were injured when an improvised explosive device detonated beneath a road culvert.
"Both victims were conscious when they were rescued and later taken to Narathiwat Hospital for treatment," he said.
Prayong said Abdullah sustained a 2cm shrapnel wound on his forehead, a severely injured left leg with an apparent deformity and head wounds believed to have been caused by flying glass fragments.
He said Yusri suffered only minor injuries and had no visible wounds.
Following the blast, security personnel from three agencies cordoned off the area and closed a 1km stretch of the road between the Sapom junction and a U-turn near a sand quarry.
"Bomb disposal (EOD) personnel and forensic officers were deployed to examine the scene.
"The Malaysian-registered vehicle involved was damaged in the blast," he added.
He said investigations into the case are ongoing.
Earlier, Sungai Golok and Tak Bai MP Amin Mat Yusof said the incident occurred when
a bomb exploded
along the route as the victim, who was driving a Proton X50, happened to pass through the area.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Australia to give regulator more power to pursue Big Tech over under-16 ban
Article:
SYDNEY: Australia will introduce new laws in parliament on Monday to strengthen its under-16 social media ban and give its internet regulator more power to pursue tech giants in court for non-compliance.
The move follows the world-first restrictions that took effect in December, a policy being watched closely by many countries seeking to emulate it as evidence suggests children are still able to access the platforms.
Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said there were still too many children on social media and tech firms were not doing enough to comply with the law.
"We're calling time on the social media companies today and doubling down on the changes that we have made and that we're prepared to make," he told reporters in Canberra.
"Today, we'll introduce legislation this afternoon that goes further to ensure social media companies are doing everything within their power to stop children under-16 being on their platforms."
The regulator is investigating possible non-compliance by five platforms: Meta's Facebook and Instagram, Snapchat , TikTok and Google's YouTube.
There was no immediate comment from Meta, Google and Snapchat. TikTok declined to comment.
Albanese called on the conservative coalition opposition to back the bill, noting the original policy passed with bipartisan support.
The changes, announced on Sunday, would double fines to A$99 million (US$68.2 million) from A$49.5 million.
They also give the eSafety Commissioner power to compel documents such as company board minutes and internal emails, ensuring legal cases being built against platforms failing to comply are as "strong as possible", said Communications Minister Anika Wells.
"My message to Big Tech is this: we are not stopping. Every effort you make to frustrate these laws will be met with our efforts to make these laws work," she said.
"If the eSafety Commissioner finds companies are not doing everything they can to comply, they will face the full force of the law."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'No more 3-hour jams': Shah Alam LRT3 line starts operations today
Article:
SHAH ALAM: The Shah Alam LRT3 line officially commenced operations today, linking commuters across Klang, Shah Alam, and Petaling Jaya.
Passengers at the Johan Setia station told the New Straits Times that the line had the potential to significantly cut both daily travel time and monthly commuting costs.
Nurshuhada Yeob, 32, who works in Petaling Jaya, said the new rail link offers a much-needed alternative to daily traffic congestion.
"It helps because I work in Petaling Jaya and deal with traffic jams every day. I hope this allows me to reach my workplace faster," she said.
She said it was a significant relief, as previously, the nearest public transport option was the KTM Komuter service, which only operated at one-hour intervals.
"Usually, getting to my workplace costs about RM7 a day in tolls, plus around RM180 a month for parking, not counting petrol money.
"But if I take this LRT, I'll just need the MY50 pass at RM50 a month. So, I'll definitely save more than RM200," she said.
She added that the line could spur further development in Johan Setia.
"I think it will really develop. With this LRT line, Johan Setia will definitely grow further," she said.
She pointed to the recent opening of KSL Esplanade Mall and a growing number of housing projects in the area as early signs.
Klang resident T. Logaraj, 35, said he had been closely following the project's progress.
"I called the helpline two weeks ago to ask when the project would be completed," he said.
Logaraj, who works in KLCC, said his previous commute had taken a heavy toll on his daily routine.
"In the past, I had to drive for two and a half to three hours in traffic to reach the office, and that really caused a troublesome commute. But with this project, it's going to make my life much easier, as we can travel with the government's support," he said.
He added that many in his situation faced the same struggle.
"So many people are travelling from Klang to KL. After work, it takes about four to five hours for some people, including me, to reach home," he said.
He welcomed the government's
transit-oriented development
plans for housing near LRT stations, saying improved connectivity could make Klang and Shah Alam more attractive places to live.
While many passengers came for their daily commute, others showed up on opening day simply to try it out.
Khoo Seng Long, 54, who lives near Bandar Utama, took the LRT from one terminal to the other to time the full journey.
The journey took about one and a half hours.
He said the train was a viable alternative to driving, though its real value would depend on the eventual post-promotion fare.
"It is better than commuting by car; if the fares are low, then it is worth it," he said.
Ahmad Syakir, who works at a cafe near the Shah Alam Stadium station, said he expects the new line to bring in more customers, including families.
"Customer traffic will increase, since it's closer to the stadium. It will definitely grow business," he said.
Iman Solihin, a barista at a cafe near the Shah Alam Seksyen 7 station, said the new stations fill a gap that had long inconvenienced commuters in the area.
"If you wanted to take the LRT before this, you'd have to go to Kelana Jaya or Subang Jaya, so it was quite far. Now that there's one at the stadium and one at Seksyen 7, it makes things much easier, especially for students," he said.
The RM16.63 billion project is expected to benefit more than two million residents along the 37.8km corridor between Bandar Utama and Johan Setia, offering seamless transfers to the Kajang and Kelana Jaya Lines.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Japan's oyster ice cream goes viral
Article:
IWATE: A soft-serve ice cream topped with fresh oysters from Yamada Bay in Japan's Iwate Prefecture has gone viral after drawing crowds this summer, Oddity Central reported.
The roadside station Michino-eki Yamada has reportedly been flooded with visitors eager to try the unusual dessert.
The dish features creamy, milk-based soft-serve ice cream paired with fresh oysters sourced locally from Yamada Bay.
It is then finished with a drizzle of soy sauce, creating an unexpected flavour combination.
The combination has been described as a "refreshing seafood taste with the sweetness of milk and the saltiness of soy sauce", surprising many.
Images and clips of the dessert circulating on social media have sparked disbelief among users.
Many initially thought the creation was generated by artificial intelligence (AI) rather than something real.
"This has gone beyond the realm of desserts!" one user wrote on X.
"Japanese creativity knows no limits!" another commented.
Michino-eki Yamada is expected to continue offering the oyster-flavoured soft-serve throughout the summer season.
A cone is priced at 600 yen (about RM15), considered affordable by local standards.
Japan's fascination with soft-serve ice cream has previously produced other unusual flavours, including matcha-coated varieties and ramen-inspired ice cream cones.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: SME optimism holds for 2026 as costs continue to rise – UOB
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Most small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) remain upbeat about their business prospects in 2026, but rising cost pressures continue to weigh on sentiment, according to the UOB Business Outlook Study 2026.
The study found that 77 per cent of SMEs are optimistic about the year ahead, although confidence is more subdued among smaller firms.
Despite the positive outlook, nearly 30 per cent of SMEs continue to face pressure from rising operating expenses, higher labour costs and increased interest rates, prompting many to adopt stricter cost-management measures.
According to UOB, persistent cost pressures are reshaping business priorities this year, with energy management emerging as an increasingly important focus area for companies seeking to improve operational efficiency and manage expenses.
The study found that around three in four businesses view energy management and efficiency as important, with the figure rising to 84 per cent among firms in the manufacturing, engineering and industrial sectors.
Among the key priorities identified were reducing energy consumption (48 per cent), lowering energy costs (44 per cent) and ensuring a stable and reliable energy supply (40 per cent).
UOB Malaysia chief executive officer Ng Wei Wei said businesses continue to face cost-related challenges, including higher energy and fuel expenses, making energy management a practical priority for many companies.
She noted that medium-sized enterprises are generally better positioned to implement efficiency initiatives, while smaller businesses often require additional support to manage financial constraints and operational pressures.
To assist SMEs, UOB Malaysia has expanded its financing support similarly to the Bank Negara Malaysia SME Stabilisation Relief Facility, aimed at helping businesses manage cash flow challenges and sustain operations amid evolving market conditions and external disruptions.
The study also highlighted growing adoption of cost-saving technologies among businesses.
About two in five companies have already installed solar panels and energy-efficient LED lighting systems, driven by their ability to reduce electricity consumption and operating costs.
In addition, SMEs are showing increasing interest in digital tools that can improve energy efficiency, lower energy expenses and support more reliable day-to-day operations.
UOB said the findings indicate that while businesses remain broadly resilient, many are balancing growth ambitions with efforts to control costs and improve operational sustainability in an increasingly challenging economic environment.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia's fund management industry poised to top US$300bil
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's fund management industry is expected to surpass US$300 billion in assets under management (AUM) in the second half of 2026 and into 2027.
This will be supported by ample domestic liquidity, government initiatives, a stronger ringgit and wider distribution channels, according to Fitch Ratings.
The ratings agency said retail investors are likely to remain key contributors, underpinned by a firm labour market, rising wages and the progressive wage model, while institutional participation also remains substantial.
Fitch said the conflict involving Iran has had limited direct impact on the industry, as most investors and assets are domestically focused.
It noted that macro stress tests conducted by the Securities Commission in 2025 showed investment funds are resilient and able to withstand redemption shocks.
Government initiatives continue to support the sector, including tax exemptions for Islamic and Sustainable and Responsible Investment funds until the 2027 assessment year.
The mandatory contribution of foreign workers to the Employees Provident Fund (EPF) from the fourth quarter of 2025 is also expected to boost savings inflows into the industry.
Fitch said total industry AUM rose 9.5 per cent year-on-year to RM1.1 trillion (US$283 billion) at the end of the first quarter of 2026, equivalent to about 60 per cent of Malaysia's GDP.
Malaysia remains among the world's largest domiciles for Islamic funds., it added.
Islamic funds accounted for 23.9 per cent of industry AUM, with assets growing 19.5 per cent year-on-year, outpacing the 6.8 per cent growth recorded by conventional funds.
Despite the strong outlook, Fitch said the industry faces increasing competition from alternative investment products, including voluntary EPF contributions and digital investment platforms offering lower-cost products.
The sector has also seen a rise in asset manager consolidation amid intensifying competition, higher operating costs and mounting fee pressures.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 32,800 completed homes worth RM16.4bil remain unsold nationwide
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A total of 32,800 completed residential units, with a combined value of RM16.37 billion, remained unsold as of the first quarter (Q1) of this year.
Deputy Housing and Local Government Minister Datuk Aiman Athirah Sabu said that of the total, 15,400 units, or 46.9 per cent, were homes priced at RM300,000 and below.
"This indicates that the remaining 53.1 per cent are homes priced above RM300,000.
"The situation shows that the issue of unsold homes is not confined to affordable housing, but is also driven by a mismatch between supply and demand across various segments of the housing market," she said during the question-and-answer session in the Dewan Rakyat today.
She was responding to a question from Datuk Willie Mongin (GPS-Puncak Borneo), who sought to know the number of unsold affordable homes, their price categories, and the actual homeownership rate among those aged 35 and below.
Aiman Athirah said the homeownership rate among low-income households in Malaysia stands at 76.3 per cent, adding that addressing homeownership challenges faced by youths and first-time homebuyers requires a more comprehensive approach.
To that end, she said the ministry is strengthening the development of an integrated national housing data repository to support more accurate, data-driven planning and policymaking.
"Through the new National Housing Policy, which is being finalised, the government is placing emphasis on providing housing that is more responsive to the needs of the people, strengthening the housing financing ecosystem, enhancing the integrated housing database, and reducing the mismatch between housing supply and market demand," she said.
In her reply to a supplementary question from Willie on rising construction costs and building material prices, Aiman Athirah said the pricing of affordable homes is not determined solely by construction costs, but also takes into account the need to strike a balance between housing affordability for the public and the sustainability of property development by developers.
"The Housing and Local Government Ministry has already undertaken affordable housing mapping using median household income data by state and district, based on the Household Income and Basic Amenities Survey 2024 published by the Department of Statistics Malaysia," she said.
She said the mapping uses the median multiple methodology to determine house price ranges that are genuinely affordable based on the purchasing power of residents in each locality. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor polls: Umno man wishes PN brother well in Kahang fight
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Former Tebrau Umno division chief Datuk Maulizan Bujang wishes his younger brother, Mazlan, who is contesting under Perikatan Nasional (PN), success in the Johor election despite their differing political affiliations in the Kahang seat.
Maulizan, who is backing the Barisan Nasional candidate for Puteri Wangsa, said he has never interfered with his brother's political stance, even as the latter contests under a different party.
"He has contested under different party tickets before. It is quite common, and we cannot stop him from standing by the principles he believes in.
"Even though we belong to different political parties, we will always remain brothers," he said.
Mazlan left Umno for Bersatu in 2018 before quitting the party four years ago.
He recently joined Pas and was nominated as its candidate in Kahang under the PN banner.
He was among the politicians who
switched parties
to seek fresh mandates from voters in the 16th Johor polls, set for July 11.
Maulizan said his brother is in his prayers and hopes he will be successful in the three-cornered fight in Kahang against Barisan Nasional's V. Rugendran and Pakatan Harapan's Imam Sabri.
"I pray that he succeeds and campaigns well," he said.
Meanwhile, Mazlan said contesting in Kahang offers a different experience compared with his previous outings, as it is a rural constituency.
He pledged to champion community development in Kahang if given the mandate by voters.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: TPK Park enters record books as largest home and living retail cluster
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: TPK Park, formerly known as Taman Perindustrian Kinrara, has been recognised by the Malaysia Book of Records as the country's largest home and living retail cluster within an industrial park.
The recognition marks the culmination of a three-decade transformation that has seen the industrial park evolve into a retail destination housing complementary home and living businesses under one location.
Today, the cluster spans 96,728 sq ft and comprises 16 businesses across eight categories.
They are interior design and renovation services, kitchens, cabinetry, flooring, furniture and soft furnishings.
TPK Park managing director Datuk Wong Shung Yen said the recognition reflected a long-term effort to create a business ecosystem that benefits both tenants and consumers.
"Business clusters do not become successful by chance. They are shaped over many years through careful planning, long-term investment and the deliberate curation of complementary businesses.
"For more than three decades, our focus has never been simply to attract tenants, but to build an ecosystem where every business strengthens the overall destination," he said.
Wong said homeowners benefit when related businesses operate within the same location, as it offers greater choice, easier product comparisons and access to specialised services.
"We believe this recognition is meaningful because it acknowledges the long-term vision and the collective contribution of every business that has been part of TPK Park's journey," he added.
To mark the achievement, TPK Park will host a Mid-Year Home Carnival from July 1-5.
The official presentation of the Malaysia Book of Records certificate will take place on July 5.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Government to ensure lower global oil prices translate into cheaper goods: Akmal Nasrullah
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government will continue monitoring the decline in global oil prices and ensure the lower costs are passed on to consumers through cheaper goods, Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir said.
The minister said that although Malaysia had successfully contained sharp price increases during the energy crisis through targeted subsidy programmes such as Budi Madani and the Subsidised Diesel Control System, the government's focus has now shifted to ensuring consumers benefit from the downward trend in energy prices.
"When prices show a downward trend, this is an opportunity for us to ensure the decline is reflected in line with falling energy costs.
"The Ministry of Domestic Trade and Cost of Living must ensure there is no profiteering or opportunistic pricing that continues to burden consumers, especially when operating costs have already begun to decline," he said when winding up the ministerial explanation on the global supply crisis in the Dewan Rakyat today.
Akmal Nasrullah said, besides monitoring prices, the government was also paying close attention to the financial position of micro, small and medium enterprises (MSMEs) to ensure they maintained a healthy cash flow amid economic uncertainty.
He said Bank Negara Malaysia had allocated RM5 billion under its SME Relief and Recovery Facility to support small and medium enterprises, including MSMEs affected by the ongoing conflict in the Middle East.
More than 1,000 MSMEs have used the facility, with RM700 million in financing approved, he added.
On measures to address the economic uncertainty arising from geopolitical conflicts, Akmal Nasrullah said the government would continue advancing its High Growth High Value (HGHV) agenda to strengthen the country's economic resilience and reduce reliance on low-value activities.
"We must remain fully committed to HGHV industries so that we do not rely on low- or medium-value activities indefinitely.
"For example, while Malaysia is not a newcomer to the global semiconductor industry, our strength remains concentrated in the back-end segment, which occupies a relatively low position in the overall semiconductor value chain.
"The interventions we can undertake include gaining access to intellectual property and enabling Malaysia to move into the front-end, or chip design stage, of the semiconductor industry. The game changer is happening now," he said.
Akmal Nasrullah said one of the government's key initiatives is its collaboration with ARM Ltd, under which 19 ARM technology access tokens have been awarded to produce Malaysian-designed semiconductor chips by 2030.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor election: 'Let voters decide my capability,' says Orang Asli candidate
Article:
MERSING: As the sole Parti Orang Asli Malaysia candidate contesting in the Johor election, Jati Awang is aware that the odds are stacked against him.
The 52-year-old, however, is unfazed and said that his candidacy is about giving Orang Asli and ordinary Malaysians a new voice rather than worrying about the profile of his rivals.
He said he does not view contesting against better-known candidates in the constituency as a disadvantage, expressing confidence in the platform championed by the party.
"This is the first time an Orang Asli-based political party is contesting an election in our country. Whether we pose a challenge or not is for the voters to decide.
"We are confident in the platform we are offering for the well-being of all races and Malaysians," he told reporters when met at Kampung Orang Asli Labong here today.
Jati said that if he wins the election, he would focus on improving the people's welfare, expanding economic opportunities, developing infrastructure, enhancing education, and strengthening unity among the multiracial communities in Endau.
Now into the third day of campaigning, Jati said he has been balancing traditional and digital approaches to reach voters from all walks of life.
Besides going door-to-door and meeting fishermen, farmers, youths and senior citizens, he has also been using social media platforms such as TikTok and Facebook to deliver his campaign messages, particularly to younger voters.
The Endau seat will see a four-cornered contest involving Jati, Barisan Nasional's Alwiyah Talib, Perikatan Nasional's Hasnul Hakimi Hussien, Pakatan Harapan's Saiful Nizam Samat.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Home Ministry explores AI to predict maritime threats, to expand use of UAVs
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Home Ministry is studying the use of artificial intelligence-based predictive analytics to identify high-risk areas and forecast potential maritime threats in the northern waters of Peninsular Malaysia.
Deputy Minister Datuk Seri Dr Shamsul Anuar Nasarah said the ministry has also agreed to expand the use of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), including drones, with phased deployment planned across national waters, including Langkawi.
He said UAVs would act as a force multiplier to existing maritime and patrol assets by enabling real-time aerial surveillance, particularly over vast, high-risk areas with limited monitoring coverage.
"This technology will enhance early detection of suspicious boat movements," he told the Dewan Rakyat during Question Time today, in reply to Datuk Mohd Suhaimi Abdullah (PN-Langkawi), who asked about the readiness of security assets and personnel in Langkawi to address migrant smuggling and controlled goods trafficking, as well as the proposal to deploy drones.
The deputy minister said the initiatives are in line with the ministry's Strategic Plan 2026-2030 and its AI Strategic Plan 2026-2030.
Shamsul Anuar also said that the ministry is reviewing a proposal to establish an Integrated Forward Operating Base in Teluk Ewa, Langkawi, which would house the Malaysian Maritime Enforcement Agency (MMEA), Marine Police Force and General Operations Force.
The initiative, he said, is expected to improve operational coordination, speed up response times, and strengthen surveillance and enforcement capabilities in combating cross-border crime.
"For maritime monitoring, the MMEA is also using the Sea Surveillance System (SWASLA) to detect contacts and suspicious activities in national waters," he said.
Meanwhile, in response to a supplementary question on Asean-level discussions regarding the influx of migrants, particularly Rohingya communities, Shamsul Anuar said the government's priority remains strengthening national security through bilateral cooperation and regional enforcement.
He said Malaysia, through the MMEA, continues to work closely with neighbouring countries, including Thailand, Indonesia and Australia, to monitor foreign fishing activities and curb migrant incursions.
Providing statistics, he said the MMEA in Kedah and Perlis recorded 152 cases involving various offences in 2025, while 49 cases were recorded up to May this year.
The Marine Police Force recorded two cases under the Immigration Act involving 10 Thai nationals up to May 2026. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Syed Saddiq to learn fate in final appeal tomorrow
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Muar MP Syed Saddiq Syed Abdul Rahman will know whether he remains a free man or faces jail time over corruption-related charges when the Federal Court delivers its decision tomorrow.
The prosecution, led by deputy public prosecutor Datuk Wan Shaharuddin Wan Ladin, is seeking to reinstate the High Court ruling which sentenced the former minister to seven years behind bars, two strokes of the rotan and a RM10 million fine.
That High Court decision, on Nov 9, 2023, found
Syed Saddiq guilty
on four charges involving criminal breach of trust (CBT), dishonest misappropriation of property and money laundering linked to funds of Bersatu's youth wing, Armada.
However, the appellate court reversed the decision on the grounds that the prosecution failed to establish dishonesty and that key findings by the trial judge could not stand.
The Federal Court hearing itself took on an unusual tone last December when a three-member bench, led by Court of Appeal president Datuk Seri Abu Bakar Jais, directed both sides to revisit and reconsider their positions, a rare
judicial pause
that underscored the weight of the case.
The prosecution was later instructed by the Attorney-General's Chambers to proceed with the appeal, setting the stage for the final legal showdown at the apex court.
For Syed Saddiq, who recently got engaged to singer-actress Bella Astillah, the stakes extend far beyond the courtroom.
A reinstatement of his conviction could not only cost him his seat as Muar MP under Article 48 of the Federal Constitution but also derail the political career of one of the country's most prominent young politicians.
For the prosecution, the appeal represents a final push to restore the High Court's findings. For the defence, it is a bid to preserve what they argue is a "very strong" acquittal already affirmed by the appellate court.
Wan Shaharuddin said he had done his best in the interest of justice and would leave the outcome of the prosecution's final appeal to the top court.
Meanwhile, senior lawyer Datuk Hisyam Teh Poh Teik, who represented the former Muda president, is confident the apex court will dismiss the prosecution's final appeal.
He said the defence's position as the respondent was firmly grounded in both the facts and the law.
The High Court had sentenced Syed Saddiq to three years' jail and one stroke of the cane for abetting CBT involving RM1 million in Armada funds.
He was also sentenced to two years' jail and one stroke of the cane for misappropriating RM120,000 from ABBE's Maybank Islamic account.
For the two money laundering charges, he was sentenced to two years' jail and fined RM10 million, with all custodial sentences ordered to run concurrently.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'One-time opportunity': South Korea bets big on AI boom
Article:
HUGE demand for the components that power artificial intelligence presents South Korea with an opportunity to bolster its chip industry against rivals such as China, analysts say.
Seoul announced Monday massive investments, including for new semiconductor factories and AI data centres, led by South Korean chipmakers.
Three companies dominate the global market for producing advanced memory chips that help power AI systems: US giant Micron, and South Korea's Samsung Electronics and SK hynix.
Their profits and share prices have soared to dizzying heights, as governments and tech companies plough hundreds of billions of dollars into training and running AI tools.
"AI has not only provided big demand, it has also created shortages, and that has driven price escalation," Jim Handy, semiconductor expert at Objective Analysis, told AFP.
Spiralling prices for memory and storage chips are being passed on to consumers – with Apple this month hiking the cost of MacBooks and iPads.
The boom has also fuelled worker demands over pay packages, with Samsung averting a major strike in May by agreeing a deal on bonuses with its largest union.
South Korea has pledged to triple spending on AI this year, aiming to join the United States and China as one of the world's top AI powers.
With China in particular racing to develop its tech industry, Seoul sees the boom period as a "one-time opportunity" to close the gap, said Lian Jye Su, a chief analyst at Omdia.
"Its the perfect time" for South Korea to leverage its strategic advantage and make investments as "the AI boom might die down" and demand could regress, he told AFP.
The Financial Times reported Saturday that Apple is seeking to buy memory chips from Chinese manufacturer CXMT – a company poised to benefit from shortages, along with Taiwanese rivals. AFP has approached Apple and CXMT for comment.
Although Chinese firms benefit from lower labour costs and big domestic demand, there could be limits to the country's tech growth, Su said.
"People are less keen to... (become) overly reliant" on Chinese silicon, a factor that Korean vendors like Samsung now want to "double down on."
Established Asian chipmakers can capitalise on the AI boom, partly because they remain innovative, Handy said.
"This gives them profitability that helps to produce a moat between them and smaller firms" who cannot maintain the same level of spending and research investment, he said.
With Monday's announcements, South Korean chipmakers want to use their current abundant cash to help diversify their offerings, Su added.
That can help them avoid becoming too dependent on the current hot sector – memory chips – in what economists call a "Dutch disease", referring to the negative effect of a temporary upswing in the price of one commodity.
The head-spinning speed of growth in the sector – Samsung's share price has risen more than 430 per cent over the past year, with SK hynix's up 770 per cent – has raised concern over how long the AI boom can last.
Some analysts such as Su are optimistic that demand will stay buoyant, given the deepening integration of AI tools into business operations.
For memory chips, "there's little to stop price rises until they impact end markets," Handy said.
"If prices rise too high then markets move to another technology or disappear altogether," he explained.
"We're not there yet."
* The article is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Deputy transport minister: Shah Alam LRT line cost cuts won't cause overcrowding
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The 2018 decision to cut costs for the Shah Alam LRT Line, previously known as LRT3, will not result in overcrowded coaches during peak hours, said the Deputy Transport Minister Datuk Hasbi Habibollah.
"According to the planning, the second phase (of the Shah Alam Line) involves adding five more stations and seven more train sets.
"So the platforms that exist now, which accommodate three coaches, are all already designed such that it's just a matter of time if (an expansion) is needed.
"Following an earlier study, the six-coach platforms were reduced to four, but the trains now run with three. So that means there is indeed room for expansion by 2030," he told the Dewan Rakyat today.
Hasbi, who is also Limbang member of Parliament, was responding to a supplementary question by Onn Abu Bakar (PH-Batu Pahat), who asked whether the cost-cutting measures — which included reducing the number of train sets from 42 to 22 — would lead to the kind of congestion seen on the Kelana Jaya LRT line.
Meanwhile, Hasbi said the project has the capacity to accommodate an estimated 223,560 passengers a day, with an estimated 6,210 passengers per hour per direction.
"Among other things, this puts the projected daily passenger ridership at 67,000 users a day in the year the LRT3 service opens," he said.
He was responding to a question from Muhammad Fawwaz Mohamad Jan (PN-Permatang Pauh) regarding delays and cost increases in the implementation of the LRT project.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: LBS Foundation rolls out 'Project AKAR' to support schools, students
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: LBS Bina Group Bhd has launched Project AKAR, a long-term school adoption programme under its philanthropic arm, LBS Foundation, beginning with Sekolah Kebangsaan Sungei Way in Petaling Jaya.
The initiative forms part of the group's corporate social responsibility (CSR) efforts to strengthen school communities and support the holistic development of students through improvements in infrastructure, education and sustainability.
The launch was officiated by Selangor state executive councillor for Islamic affairs and innovation culture Dr Mohammad Fahmi Ngah, together with LBS Foundation committee members led by Tan Sri Dr Lim Hock San, as well as school administrators, teachers and students.
The ceremony also featured a symbolic tree-planting session to underscore the programme's focus on sustainability.
Mohammad Fahmi lauded the initiative and expressed his appreciation to LBS Foundation, describing it as a commendable and impactful effort that would bring meaningful benefits to the wider community, particularly in strengthening education and youth development.
He added that such initiatives play an important role in uplifting community well-being and fostering long-term social impact.
He also called for greater collaboration between the corporate sector and educational institutions.
Project AKAR, short for awareness, knowledge, actions, and resilience, is designed as a long-term initiative aimed at nurturing future generations through a school adoption programme.
Under the programme, the LBS Foundation will adopt one school in every three-year cycle, with each cycle centred on three key pillars – infrastructure, learning, and sustainability.
These focus areas are designed to enhance the school environment, enrich learning opportunities, and cultivate sustainable practices that create lasting benefits for students and the wider school community.
As the programme's first beneficiary, SK Sungei Way has received stationery and back-to-school supplies, while repairs have been carried out on key school facilities, including doors and staircase railings.
LBS Foundation chairman Tan Sri Dr Lim Hock San said Project AKAR reflects the foundation's belief that lasting social change begins with education.
"At LBS Foundation, we believe that meaningful change begins at the community level, especially through education. 'AKAR', which means 'roots', symbolises the children who represent the foundation of our future. Therefore, Project AKAR reflects our commitment to nurturing young minds and creating opportunities that go beyond the classroom, ensuring that every child has the support and environment needed to thrive," Lim said.
The foundation said it plans to expand the programme by adopting more schools in future, reinforcing its commitment to community development through education.
Aligned with its education pillar, LBS Foundation plays a pivotal role in strengthening educational outcomes and promoting a safe, inclusive, and supportive learning environment by working closely with school teachers to identify key areas of need, enhance student engagement, and implement structured interventions that prioritise student well-being, safety, and holistic development.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: M'sian injured after X50 hit by bomb blast in Thailand
Article:
KOTA BARU: A Malaysian man was injured after his vehicle was caught in a bomb blast in Kampung Sapom, Tak Bai, Thailand, this afternoon.
The 38-year-old victim suffered injuries to his body, while his vehicle was also damaged in the explosion.
He is currently receiving treatment at Narathiwat Hospital.
Sungai Golok and Tak Bai member of Parliament Amin Mat Yusof said the incident occurred when a bomb exploded along the route as the victim, who was driving a Proton X50, happened to pass through the area.
"The victim sustained injuries and was sent to Narathiwat Hospital for further treatment.
"His vehicle was also damaged in the explosion," he said.
Meanwhile, Kelantan police chief Datuk Mohd Yusoff Mamat, when contacted, confirmed that police had received information about a Malaysian driving a Malaysian-registered vehicle who was injured in a bomb blast.
"I do not have further details on whether the bomb was planted on the vehicle or placed by the roadside.
"The victim is from Sabah," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Global supply crisis may push fertiliser costs up 20pct
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Fertiliser costs are projected to rise by 15 to 20 per cent amid ongoing global supply disruptions, Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir said.
He said 63 per cent of Malaysia's fertiliser supply is imported, leaving the sector vulnerable to global price shocks.
"Based on current assessments, fertiliser costs are projected to rise by 15 to 20 per cent, while animal feed costs are expected to increase by around 8 per cent," he told the Dewan Rakyat during a briefing on measures to address the global supply crisis.
Akmal said rising logistics and agricultural input costs were putting pressure on food prices.
He said the government was therefore focusing on ensuring the supply of essential goods, controlling the prices of basic necessities and improving the efficiency of distribution channels.
The wider supply disruption has also put pressure on the logistics, aviation, shipping, agriculture, manufacturing, construction and trade sectors.
"Industries are facing surging fuel costs, delays in the delivery of intermediate inputs, rising inventory costs and challenges in securing alternative sources of supply," he said.
The global supply disruption stems from the conflict between the United States and Iran, which, according to the government, has disrupted shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
Around 20 per cent of the world's oil supply and liquefied natural gas (LNG) passes through the strategic waterway.

Title: Court of Appeal rules survivor cannot alter mutual will unilaterally
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Court of Appeal has ruled that the survivor of a mutual will cannot unilaterally alter the agreed distribution of an estate after the death of the other party.
In a 2-1 majority decision, the three-member bench chaired by Datuk Mohamed Zaini Mazlan held that the doctrine of mutual wills became binding upon the death of the first testator.
Consequently, the surviving party could not unilaterally alter the agreed distribution of the estate through a subsequent will.
Zaini said the survivor became a constructive trustee of the property upon the death of the other party and was legally bound to honour the agreement embodied in the mutual wills.
He sat with Datuk Ismail Ibrahim, who concurred, and K. Muniandy, who dissented.
The ruling arose from an appeal involving the estate of the late Dennis Charles Sen, who executed substantially identical wills with his first wife, Jaspal Kaur, in 2007.
The couple, who had no children, agreed that their jointly owned double-storey terrace house in USJ, Subang Jaya, would eventually be inherited by their niece, Karina Narveen Kaur Gill.
Following Jaspal's death in 2008, Dennis inherited her share of the property. He later married Zhao Jingkun in 2012 and executed a new will in 2014, revoking all previous wills and leaving his estate, including the Subang Jaya house, to Zhao.
After Dennis died in 2019, Charanjeet Kaur Sukhbir Singh, who had been appointed executor under the 2007 wills, challenged the validity of the 2014 will, arguing that the earlier wills were mutual wills and therefore irrevocable after Jaspal's death.
The High Court dismissed the claim, ruling that the 2007 wills were merely mirror wills and that Dennis was entitled to execute a new will.
However, the appellate court reversed the decision after finding sufficient evidence that Dennis and Jaspal intended the 2007 wills to operate as mutual wills.
Zaini said the absence of an express clause stating that the wills were irrevocable did not prevent the court from finding that they were mutual wills.
"We respectfully disagree with the learned judge's finding that the wills could not constitute mutual wills merely because they did not contain the word 'irrevocable'.
"The absence of that expression is not, by itself, determinative. What is paramount is the intention of the deceased and his first wife at the time the wills were executed.
"In the present case, the evidence plainly pointed to a common intention that the wills were to operate as mutual wills," he said in the recently released grounds of judgment.
The court held that Dennis was bound to honour the terms of the mutual wills executed by him and his first wife and could not act contrary to her wishes.
It ruled that Dennis could not defeat that obligation by executing the 2014 will in Zhao's favour and ordered that the estate be administered in accordance with the 2007 mutual wills.
In his dissenting judgment, Muniandy said the 2007 wills were merely mirror wills as there was no written agreement or express irrevocability clause establishing a legally binding contract between Dennis and Jaspal.
He further held that Dennis' remarriage in 2012 revoked the 2007 will under Section 12 of the Wills Act and that the 2014 will represented his final testamentary intention, leaving no basis for appellate intervention.
Lawyers Annera Joshini Chowdury and Lu Yiing Suey appeared for Charanjeet, while Teo Wee Shiuan and Nur Dalila Zulkarnain represented Zhao.

Title: Johor election: Layang-Layang BN candidate stresses unity as core campaign pillar
Article:
SIMPANG RENGGAM: Barisan Nasional candidate for Layang-Layang Chua Jian Boon is placing strong emphasis on unity among Johor's multiracial communities, saying development must go hand in hand with social cohesion.
Chua said economic progress would lose its meaning if harmony among the state's main communities is not preserved.
"There is no point in chasing development and progress in Johor if we fail to maintain social cohesion, especially unity among the three main races in the state," he told the New Straits Times.
The special officer to caretaker Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi said his approach throughout the campaign has been to engage closely with all communities, regardless of background, adding that he is comfortable participating in multicultural events and grassroots activities.
He cited his participation in a Bubur Asyura programme at a local surau as an example of his grassroots engagement, in which he joined community members in food preparation.
"I am comfortable interacting with all communities, whether Malay, Chinese or Indian. I have no difficulty participating in their cultural and community activities."
Chua said his upbringing and exposure to diverse communities have shaped his ability to connect naturally with voters from different backgrounds.
"The main agenda I am bringing is unity among races."
Chua is set for a three-cornered fight with Pakatan Harapan (PH) and Perikatan Nasional (PN).
He will face the incumbent, Abd Mutalip Abd Rahim, who is contesting as a PN candidate after quitting Umno, as well as PH candidate Guna Balakrishnan.
The semi-rural constituency under the Simpang Renggam parliamentary seat has a total of 25,181 registered voters, according to Election Commission data as of May 2026.
The constituency's voter composition consists of 55 per cent Malay, 30 per cent Chinese, 13 per cent Indian and the remainder from other communities, making cross-ethnic support a key factor for any candidate.
Chua, who previously contested the Kulai parliamentary seat in the 15th General Election, said he is aware of the pressure as attention is focused on whether BN can retain the traditional seat.
He said the campaign is anchored on strong grassroots unity and the enduring "Bangsa Johor" spirit that underpins the coalition's machinery.
Chua acknowledged the responsibility placed on him but said confidence in retaining the seat is driven by strong multiracial support within the BN component parties.
"My main task is to bring the spirit of unity, an ambassador of unity for this state. We want to prove that regardless of race or constituency, we remain united in ensuring victory."
He added that his campaign remains focused on unity, stability and continuity of development in Layang-Layang.
The Johor election will be held on July 11, with early voting scheduled for July 7.
